content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED BY 1998
AMENDMENTS TO
REGULATION 18530
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
October 31, 1997
3. Arnoldo Beltràn
Beltràn Leal & Medina
221 North Figueroa Street, Suite 1000
Los Angeles, California 90012
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-509
Dear Mr. Beltràn:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Your firm serves as the City Attorney for the City of Bell
Gardens, and you are seeking advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Act.
QUESTION
Under the Act, may the City of Bell Gardens (“the City”) prepare, produce and distribute
a newsletter, at public expense, which references the city council, but does not make reference to
or include a photograph of any individual elected officer of the City?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Under the Act, the City may prepare, produce and distribute a newsletter, at public
expense, which references the city council, but does not make reference to or include a
photograph of any individual elected officer of the City at public expense without violating the
Act.
1 Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 3 Street, Suite 450, Sacramento. CA 958l4.P. 0. Box 807. Sacramentn flA QcRl 2• (91 6


File No. A-97-509
Page 2
FACTS
The City seeks to prepare, produce and distribute at public expense a newsletter titled
“Accomplishments.” The newsletter contains information regarding accomplishments by the
City in the year 1997. The newsletter was prepared in consultation with elected officers ofthe
City. The newsletter contains the term “city council” but does not make reference to any
individual elected officer ofthe City. You have provided a copy of the newsletter for our review.
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 of the Act provides: “No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense.” A mass mailing is defmed by Section 82041.5 which provides:
“Mass mailing’ means over two hundred substantially similar
pieces of mail, but does not include a form letter or other mail
which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other
inquiry.”
The facts do not indicate how many newsletters will be distributed or to whom.
2
However, based on the content of the newsletter, it appears that the newsletter will be mailed to
the City’s residents. The newsletter would qualify as a mass mailing under section 82041.5 if the
City intends to mail over two hundred copies ofthe newsletter to the City’s residents.
Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing at public expense is prohibited only if all of
the following apply:
“(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at
his or her residence, place of employment or business, or post
office box. For purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
(2) the item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the mailing, or
2 The Commission does not act as the fmder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71.)


File No. A-97-509
Page 3
(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer.
(3)(A) Any ofthe costs of distribution is paid for
with public moneys; or
(B) Costs of design, production, and printing
exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and
the design, production, or printing is done with the
intent of sending the item other than as pennitted by
this regulation.
(4) More than two hundred substantially similar
items are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding
any item sent in response to an unsolicited request
and any item described in subdivision (b).”
If an item does not include the name, photograph or any reference to an elected officer
who is affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the mailing, the item is not subject
to the restrictions of the regulation. (Regulation 18901(a)(2); Owen Advice Letter, No. 1-90-
704.) The Commission has interpreted that a general reference to a “city council” is not a
reference to an individual councilmember. (Owen Advice Letter, supra.) For purposes of
Regulation 18901 (a)(2)(A), if a city council is producing and sending a mailing, then the city is
the agency that produces or sends the document. (Zaragosa Advice Letter, No. A-90-062.)
Additionally, ifthe mailing has been prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination
or concert with an elected officer, any use ofthe elected officer’s name, photograph or office, or
any reference to the officer is prohibited. (Regulation 18901 (a)(2)(B).)
The newsletter you have provided does not include the name or photograph of an elected
officer who is affiliated with the City. There is a general reference to the city council; however,
this does not constitute a reference to an individual councilmember. While the newsletter would
be prepared in consultation with the city council, Regulation 1890 1(a)(2)(B) is not violated
because no individual councilmember’s name, photograph, office or any reference to an
individual councilmember is present.


File No. A-97-509
Page 4
However, you should be aware that Section 85300 provides that, “no public officer shall
expend and no candidate shall accept any public moneys for the purpose of seeking elective
office.” Therefore where public moneys are spent to advocate or promote a candidate’s election
to public office, we have advised that Section 85300 has been violated. (Lowder Advice Letter,
No. A-95-223.) However, as a general rule, Section 85300 would not be applicable where there
is no “express advocacy” for an elected official’s reelection. (Lowder Advice Letter, supra.)
Since you would like to use public funds to distribute the newsletter, the content of the
newsletter must comport with Section 85300. Based on the copy of the newsletter you have
submitted there does not appear to be any “express advocacy” for an elected official’s
nomination, election, or the defeat ofhis or her opponents.
However, at the bottom ofthe
second page the language, “We are here to serve you,” generally references the City Council’s
role of serving the community as their elected officials. This general reference does not appear to
be connected to expressly advocating either an individual Councilmember’s nomination or
reelection, or the defeat of his or her opponents, which is prohibited pursuant to Section 85300.
However, in future newsletters care must be taken to comport with Section 85300 as discussed
above.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
aJ tS’
By:
Alisa Fong
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division
